response:
Module 1: Identify the equilibrium population size $ P_e $  
The equilibrium population size is found when $ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0 $, so:  
$$ rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) = 0 $$  
Substituting $ r = 0.1 $, $ K = 1000 $, and $ h(t) = 10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $:  
$$ 0.1P\left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) - 10 - 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) = 0 $$  
Solving for $ P $ when the population is constant:  
$$ P_e = \frac{K}{1 + \frac{h(t)}{r}} = \frac{1000}{1 + \frac{10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)}{0.1}} $$  
$$ P_e = \frac{1000}{1 + 100 + 50\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)} = \frac{1000}{101 + 50\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)} $$  

Module 2: Analyze the stability of the equilibrium population  
The stability of $ P_e $ depends on the sign of the derivative of $ \frac{dP}{dt} $ at $ P = P_e $.  
The derivative of $ \frac{dP}{dt} $ is:  
$$ \frac{d^2P}{dt^2} = r\left(1 - \frac{2P}{K}\right) - \frac{dh}{dt} $$  
At $ P = P_e $, $ \frac{d^2P}{dt^2} $ determines stability.  
If $ \frac{d^2P}{dt^2} < 0 $, the equilibrium is stable; if $ > 0 $, it is unstable.  

Module 3: Determine the time when the population is no longer sustainable
